title: Living in a society
description:
We're living in a society! Rules. Norms. The faint expectation of decency.

## Success Criteria
- Behavior consistent with the premise that we are, in fact, living in a society.
- Reduced number of incidents per outing (baseline TBD).

title: Escape Greek-restaurant bathroom (door handle 'looked experimental')
description:
Trusted Christian when he said the door handle 'looked experimental.' Am now locked in. Blocked on a locksmith, or on Christian remembering I exist.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Enter bathroom.
2. Trust Christian.

## Acceptance Criteria
- George is on the correct side of the door.

title: Christian insists he is the faster swimmer — gather evidence
description:
Christian keeps claiming he is the faster swimmer using a 'revolutionary new method.' This cannot stand.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Agree to a friendly race.
2. Lose, inexplicably.
3. Listen to Christian explain the 'method' for 40 minutes.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Stopwatch data, witnessed by Steve, establishing George as the faster swimmer.
- Christian's 'method' is shown to be him pushing off the wall extra hard.

title: Ranch is not salad dressing?
description:
You heard me, Christian!

## Decision
It is.

title: HR Complaint: Business casual
description: Listen, I know the guys on the 8th floor get to do pretty much whatever they want but open toes are distracting in the workplace. Will someone please tell "Trev" that pleated pants don't balance out Birkenstocks?

title: Onboard Marybeth (the most Marybeth of all the Christians)
description: New hire. Steve's assessment, verbatim: 'Of all the Christians I know, you are the most Marybeth.' She starts Monday.

title: Lobby art: ketchup sunset vs. mustard ocean
description:
## Decision
Neither.

## Rationale
The Vandalway lobby will not smell faintly of a diner.

## Alternatives Considered
- Ketchup sunset (too warm).
- Mustard ocean (too yellow).
- A normal painting (Morgan: 'you don't understand culture').

title: Maintain the faint illusion of being a respectable adult
description:
An ongoing effort. Hampered daily by the people I choose to associate with.

## Acceptance Criteria
- One (1) outing completed without incident. (Stretch goal.)

title: Productize Christian's restroom sock-drying method
description:
Christian has a 'revolutionary new method' for drying socks in public restrooms. He is certain it scales.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A method that does not knock over a rack of women's blouses on entry.
- At least one (1) dry sock.
- Health-code review (Steve, reluctantly).

title: RSVP: Morgan's mustard-ocean gallery opening
description: Morgan invited me to a gallery opening for a man who paints exclusively with condiments. Ketchup sunsets. Mustard oceans. I am told I 'don't understand culture.'

title: Mixed contacts: nearsighted left, farsighted right
description:
## Question
Wear one nearsighted contact (left eye) and one farsighted (right eye), and just close an eye to adapt?

## Decision
Just close an eye to adapt.

## Rationale
Adaptation is free. The eyelids are right there. I already own the eyes.

## Alternatives Considered
- Monovision (cowardice).
- A mild multifocal in one eye + distance in the other (the optometrist's idea — too reasonable; rejected on principle).

title: Steve keeps saying 'What a thing to say' with zero context
description:
It happens mid-conversation, unprompted. No one knows what thing. Steve will not elaborate.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Say anything.
2. (sometimes) Steve: 'What a thing to say.'

## Acceptance Criteria
- Identify the triggering thing, OR accept it as ambient.

title: File patent: public-restroom sock drying
description: Christian wants to file before someone steals the method. Steve is not returning his calls.

title: Replace the turkey slice (dropped face-down on the floor)
description: It was the only slice. Face-down. Of course. Naturally.

title: Return the shirt with 'collar integrity issues'
description: The collar had integrity issues. It did! Return it. Maintain composure when the clerk gives The Look.

title: Does 'MONOVISION' really need two O's?
description:
## Question
The company is named MONOVISION. Mono. As in ONE. So why are there visibly TWO O's in the logo? Who approved this? I want a name.

## Decision
(pending — escalated to the guys at the Del Boca Vista Bridge Team)

## Rationale
A company with 'mono' in the name should not flaunt a second O. It's provocative.

## Alternatives Considered
- 'MONVISION' (Steve says it looks like a kitchen appliance).
- Pretend the second O is silent.
